1: qûmî 'ôrî — Arise, shine!. Both verbs are Qal imperatives feminine singular addressing Zion as a person. The imperative of 'ûr (אוֹר — to be/become light) is rare in this Qal form; more commonly the sun shines — here Zion herself is commanded to radiate. kî bā' 'ôrēk̠ — for your light has come — the Qal perfect bā' frames the light's arrival as already accomplished from the divine perspective. ûk̠ĕbôd YHWH 'ālayik̠ zāraḥ — and the glory of YHWH has risen upon you — the verb zāraḥ (זָרַח) is the sunrise word (cf. Ps 84:12); YHWH's glory rises on Zion as the sun rises over the horizon.
2: kî-hinnēh haḥōšek̠ yĕk̠assè-'āreṣ — for behold, darkness covers the earth. The verb k̠āsāh (Pi'el, yĕk̠assè — to cover completely) describes total envelopment. wa'ărāp̄el lĕ'ummîm — thick darkness the peoples. The contrast: wĕ'āl-ayik̠ yizraḥ YHWH — but upon you YHWH will rise — the same sunrise verb (zāraḥ) as v. 1, now with YHWH as subject. ûk̠ĕb̠ôdô 'ālayik̠ yērā'eh — and his glory will appear upon you.
3: wĕhāl ĕkû gôyim lĕ'ôrēk̠ — and nations shall walk by your light. The verb hālak̠ (to walk) with prepositional lĕ (toward/by) — nations navigate by Zion's light as mariners navigate by a star. ûmĕlāk̠îm lĕnōgah zarḥēk̠ — and kings to the brightness of your rising. The noun nōgah (radiance, brilliance — from נָגַהּ) and the infinitive/construct zarḥēk̠ (your sunrise). The reversal of ch. 2's darkness is complete: what was thick darkness over nations becomes a luminous beacon drawing them.
4: ś'î-sābîb̠ 'ênayik̠ ûrĕ'î — lift up your eyes around and see. The imperative ś'î (Qal imperative feminine of נָשָׂא — to lift) combined with ûrĕ'î (and see). The returnees: sons from far (mirrāḥôq) and daughters mĕ'ummānôt 'al-ṣad — carried at the side. The verb 'āman (Pi'el participle, mĕ'ummānôt) means nursed/carried — the daughters are not infants but are escorted/supported at the side (cf. Num 11:12).
5: 'āz tirĕ'î wĕnāhārtĕ — then you will see and be radiant. The verb nāhar (נָהַר) is deliberately polysemous: it means both to flow/stream (as a river) and to be radiant/shine (as a face lit with joy). Both meanings are active — Zion floods with joy and glows with it. wĕpāḥad wĕrāḥab̠ lĕb̠āb̠ēk̠ — your heart shall tremble and expand — pāḥad (to tremble with awe) and rāḥab̠ (to widen/enlarge) as simultaneous responses; awe-struck expansion.
6: šipʿat gĕmallîm tĕk̠assēk̠ — a multitude of camels shall cover you. The noun šip̄'āh (abundance, multitude) with gĕmallîm (camels) — caravans from Midian and Ephah and Sheba bringing gold (zāhāb̠) and frankincense (lĕb̠ônāh). The combination of gold and frankincense is specific; Matthew 2:11 (the Magi's gifts) draws on this passage. ûtĕhillot YHWH yĕb̠aśśērû — and they shall bring good news of the praises of YHWH — the Pi'el yĕb̠aśśērû (from בָּשַׂר — to bring good news; same root as bĕśôrāh and NT euangelion).
7: kol-ṣō'n qēdār yiqqāb̠ĕṣû lāk̠ — all the flocks of Kedar shall be gathered for you. The Niphal yiqqāb̠ĕṣû (to be gathered) as divine passive — it is YHWH who gathers them. Rams of Nebaioth ('êlê nĕb̠āyôt) shall minister on the altar. wĕb̠êt tip̄'artî 'ăp̄ā'ēr — and I will glorify my glorious house — the Piel 'ăp̄ā'ēr (from פָּאַר — to beautify, adorn, glorify) applied to the temple by YHWH himself.
8: mî-'ēlleh k̠ā'āb̠ tĕ'ûp̄ēnāh — who are these that fly like a cloud?. The image is of ships and doves converging on Zion from the sea. wĕk̠ayyônîm 'el-'ărubbōtêhem — and like doves to their windows/dovecotes — 'ărubbāh (latticed window, opening) is the word used for the windows of heaven in Gen 7:11; doves return instinctively to their home-openings.
9: kî-lî 'iyyîm yĕqawwû — for to me the coastlands wait. The verb qiwwāh (Pi'el — to wait, hope intensely) shares its root with tiqwāh (hope); the coastlands' waiting is active, expectant hope. wĕ'oniyyôt taršîš bārî'šōnāh — ships of Tarshish in the lead. lĕhābî' bānayik̠ mērāḥôq — to bring your sons from far off; zāhāb̠ām ûk̠isp̄ām — their gold and silver with them, lĕšēm YHWH 'ĕlōhayik̠ — for the name of YHWH your God.
10: ûb̠ānû b̠ĕnê-nēk̠ār ḥômōtayik̠ — foreigners shall build your walls. The noun nēk̠ār (נֵכָר — foreign, alien) marks these builders as Gentiles — the social reversal is complete. ûmalkêhem yĕšārĕtûnĕk̠ — and their kings shall serve you — the Pi'el yĕšārĕtûnĕk̠ (from שָׁרַת — to minister, serve in the sense of priestly/royal service) applied to foreign kings. The theological ground: kî b̠ĕqiṣpî hikkîtîk̠ ûb̠irĕṣônî riḥamtîk̠ — for in my wrath I struck you but in my favor I have had mercy on you.
11: wĕpit ĕḥû šĕ'ārayik̠ tāmîd — your gates shall be open continually. The combination yômām wālaylāh (day and night) with lō' yissāgĕrû (they shall not be shut) — perpetual openness is the sign of absolute security; a city without threat needs no closed gates. lĕhāb̠î' 'ēlayik̠ ḥêl gôyim — to bring to you the wealth of nations — ḥayil (חַיִל — strength, wealth, military force) of nations streams in unchecked.
12: kî-haggôy wĕhammamlek̠āh 'ăšer lō'-ya'ab̠dûk̠ yō'b̠ēdû — for the nation and kingdom that will not serve you shall perish. The Qal imperfect ya'ab̠dûk̠ (from עָבַ̠ד — to serve) versus yō'b̠ēdû (from אָבַ̠ד — to perish) — the wordplay is deliberate: not to serve Zion is to perish. wĕhaggôyim ḥāreb̠ yeḥĕrāb̠û — those nations shall be utterly laid waste.
13: kĕb̠ôd hallĕb̠ānôn 'ēlayik̠ yāb̠ô' — the glory of Lebanon shall come to you. Lebanon's timber — the finest in the ancient world — will flow to Zion. Three trees: b̠ĕrôš tiddār wĕtĕ'aššûr — cypress, plane/elm, and pine (tree identifications disputed; all are prized timber). Purpose: lĕp̄ā'ēr mĕqôm miqdāšî — to beautify the place of my sanctuary — the Piel of פָּאַר again (cf. v. 7); mĕqôm raglāy 'ăk̠abbēd — the place of my feet I will honor — the temple as YHWH's footstool.
14: wĕhāl ĕkû 'ēlayik̠ šāḥôaḥ — they shall come to you bowing down. Those who afflicted Zion (mĕ'annayik̠, Pi'el participle of עָנָה — to afflict) and despised her now prostrate themselves. The new names of Zion: 'îr YHWH — city of YHWH; ṣiyyôn qĕdôš yiśrā'ēl — Zion of the Holy One of Israel. The divine title qĕdôš yiśrā'ēl (Holy One of Israel) is the signature title of Isaiah throughout the book.
15: taḥat hĕyôtēk̠ 'ăzûb̠āh ûśĕnû'āh — instead of your being forsaken and hated. Two participles ('ăzûb̠āh — forsaken; śĕnû'āh — hated) describe Zion's former state. The reversal: wĕśamtîk̠ lĕgĕ'ôn 'ôlām — I will make you an everlasting pride — the noun gĕ'ôn (גָּאוֹן — majesty, pride) can be negative (arrogance) but here is entirely positive: Zion's splendor that endures. mĕśôś dôr wādôr — a joy from generation to generation.
16: wĕyānaqtĕ ḥălab̠ gôyim — you shall suck the milk of nations. The nursing metaphor reverses: Zion, once suckling nations for their resources, now receives their nourishment. wĕšad mĕlāk̠îm tinyāqî — and the breast of kings you shall suck — the imagery of royal wet nurses applied to Zion receiving wealth and nourishment from kings. wĕyāda'tĕ kî 'ănî YHWH môšî'ēk̠ — the recognition formula: and you will know that I am YHWH your Savior.
17: taḥat hannĕḥōšèt 'āb̠î' zāhāb̠ — instead of bronze I will bring gold. The escalating exchange formula — gold for bronze, silver for iron, bronze for wood, iron for stone — each substitution moves up in value. The officers of Zion: wĕnātattî pĕquddātēk̠ šālôm — I will appoint peace as your overseers — pĕquddāh (office, appointment) with šālôm (peace) as the officer itself; peace rules over Zion. wĕnōgĕśayik̠ ṣĕdāqāh — and righteousness as your taskmasters.
18: lō'-yiššāma' 'ôd ḥāmās bĕ'arṣēk̠ — violence shall no more be heard in your land. The Niphal imperfect yiššāma' (from שָׁמַע — to be heard) negated: not merely eliminated but inaudible. šēb̠er wāšēb̠er — devastation or destruction (a pair in Hebrew prophecy); neither present nor rumored. The walls and gates renamed: wĕqārā'tā yĕšû'āh ḥômōtayik̠ — you will call your walls Salvation; ûšĕ'ārayik̠ tĕhillāh — and your gates Praise.
19: lō'-yihyeh lāk̠ 'ôd haššèmeš lĕ'ôr yômām — the sun shall be no more your light by day. The eschatological transformation: natural luminaries superseded by YHWH himself. kî YHWH yihyeh lāk̠ lĕ'ôr 'ôlām — YHWH will be your everlasting light. The word 'ôlām (עוֹלָם — everlasting, age-enduring) qualifies the light as belonging to the new age where temporal cycles end. wĕ'ĕlōhayik̠ lĕtip̄'artēk̠ — and your God for your glory/beauty. NT: Rev 21:23 applies this verse to the New Jerusalem where the Lamb is its lamp.
20: lō'-yāb̠ô' 'ôd šimšēk̠ — your sun shall no more go down. The verse doubles the promise of v. 19 with additional negations: the moon will not withdraw (yē'āsēp̄, Niphal of אָסַף — to be gathered in). kî YHWH yihyeh lāk̠ lĕ'ôr 'ôlām — the exact repetition from v. 19 is emphatic: YHWH as everlasting light is the irreversible new reality. wĕšālĕmû yĕmê 'eb̠lēk̠ — and the days of your mourning shall end — šālēm (to be complete, finished) applied to mourning: grief reaches its terminus.
21: wĕ'ammēk̠ kullām ṣaddîqîm — and your people shall all be righteous. The universal qualifier kullām (all of them) is the most sweeping covenant inclusion imaginable; no subset is excluded. lĕ'ôlām yîrĕšû 'āreṣ — they shall inherit the land forever — the covenant promise to Abraham framed in its ultimate horizon. nēṣer maṭṭā'ay — a shoot/branch of my planting — the noun nēṣer (shoot, branch, from the same root as נָצַר — to watch, guard) will recur in 11:1 for the messianic shoot from the stump of Jesse. ma'ăśēh yādāy lĕhitpā'ēr — the work of my hands that I might be glorified.
22: haqqāṭōn yihyeh lĕ'elep̄ — the least shall become a thousand. The smallest unit becomes a military thousand-unit; wĕhaṣṣā'îr lĕgôy 'āṣûm — and the smallest a mighty nation. The paradox of the closing line: 'ănî YHWH bĕ'ittāh 'ăḥîšennāh — I YHWH will hasten it in its time. The two phrases appear contradictory: bĕ'ittāh (in its appointed time — YHWH's sovereign calendar) and 'ăḥîšennāh (Hiphil of חוּשׁ — I will hasten it). The tension is theological: YHWH's eschatological purposes both have a fixed time and are subject to his sovereign acceleration. The chapter ends on this open dynamism.
